macro_segments.title: Introduction to Daf 27 and Review of Previous Discussions on Shevuot Categories

micro_segments.title: Overview of daf 27 and parameters of binding oaths (Korban liability)
Review of Rabbi Akiva vs. Rabbi Yishmael debate on past oaths and Korban
Discussion of positive and negative formulation requirement (Lehara o Leheitiv)
Distinction between Shevuot and Nedarim; Shogeg (unintentional) oath category
Requirement that oath must be expressed aloud (Levatay Bisifatayim); binding nature of silent acceptances

macro_segments.title: Mishna: Oath to Negate or Fulfill Mitzvah; Rabbi Yehuda Ben Batera's Position

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Nishba levatel et hamitzvah (oath to negate mitzvah) is not binding; exemption from Korban
Mishna: Oath to fulfill mitzvah (Lekayem); logical expectation for obligation vs. actual ruling of exemption
Introduction of Rabbi Yehuda Ben Batera's position: oath to fulfill mitzvah should be binding
Rabbis' response: Lehara o Leheitiv requires reversibility (Lav v'Hein); cannot negate mitzvah so cannot fulfill it
Tosafot's interpretation: Distinction between negative and positive mitzvot; Ein Issur Chal Al Issur vs. Ein Shevua Chala Al Shevua
Tosafot's girsa: removing Kaf; Rabbi Yehuda Ben Batera permits oath to fulfill positive mitzvah but not negative mitzvah
Explanation of double obligation vs. double forbiddenness; Can one be doubly obligated vs. doubly forbidden?

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Derivation that Lehara o Leheitiv refers to discretionary matters, not mitzvot realm

micro_segments.title: Tanu Rabbanan: Attempt to include oath to negate mitzvah; exclusion via Lehara o Leheitiv (good and bad must be discretionary)
Derivation that Hatavah (good) refers to neutral field via parallel with Harah (bad); exclusion of oath to negate mitzvah
Derivation that Harah (bad) refers to neutral field via parallel with Hatavah; exclusion of oath to fulfill mitzvah

macro_segments.title: Analysis of Lehara o Leheitiv: Self-injury, Injury to Others, and Doing Good

micro_segments.title: Possible oath to self-injury: is it binding? Gemara implies self-injury is permissible (contrary to later halacha)
Oath to harm others is not binding because bad in Lehara o Leheitiv cannot mean to violate mitzvah (lo tashmor)
Oath to do good to others is binding; reversal (won't do good) falls into neutral zone, not forbidden
Three-zone model: Good, Neutral, Bad; oath to avoid harm doesn't work (opposite is harm); oath to do good works (opposite is neutral)

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Derivation that Lehara o Leheitiv refers to discretionary matters, not mitzvot realm (continued)

micro_segments.title: Gemara's challenge: Circularity of the derivation; both good and bad could refer to mitzvah realm
Gemara's resolution: The word Or (Lehara o Leheitiv) includes doing good to others, proving we exclude oath to negate mitzvah
If verse were about mitzvot, oath to harm others would be binding; since Or teaches good to others works, we know oath to negate mitzvah excluded
Distinction between Rabbi Yochanan (Vav reads as Or) and Rabbi Yoshiya (Vav reads as And, Or is needed to make it Or)

macro_segments.title: Mishna and Gemara: Multiple Oaths on the Same Object; Principle of Ein Shevua Chala Al Shevua

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Oath not to eat loaf stated three ways; violation results in only one Korban obligation
Gemara: Why state three times? Teaches that second oath does not take effect due to Ein Shevua Chala Al Shevua
Rava's principle: Wording (Ochel vs. Ochalnah) indicates scope; Ochalnah means entire loaf, Ochel means each piece
Order matters: If oath forbidding more comes second, it doesn't take effect; if it comes first, broader prohibition can take effect
Rava: Second oath remains present but ineffective; if first oath is retroactively annulled via Hetter, second takes effect retroactively
Tosafot's girsa: Only two oaths in Mishna; derives principle from phrase Eino Chayav Ela Achas implying second oath still hovers
Proof from Nazir case: If one uproots first Nezirut via Sheila, second Nezirut takes retroactive effect with its Korban
Gemara's challenge: Nazir case differs because second Nezirut always remains present; with oaths, second has no effect if first not nullified

topical_tags.term: Lehara o Leheitiv (positive and negative formulation)
Shevuat Bitui (future-oriented oath with Korban consequence)
Shevuat Shav (oath for naught; lashes but no Korban)
Ein Shevua Chala Al Shevua (oath does not take effect on oath)
Ein Issur Chal Al Issur (forbiddenness does not take effect on forbiddenness)
Mushba V'Omed MeHar Sinai (already sworn from Mount Sinai; one cannot be doubly bound)
Tosafot's distinction: negative vs. positive mitzvah
Rava's principle on oath scope and wording
Hetter Neder / Hetter Shevuah (annulment of vow/oath)
Second oath hovering and retroactive effect
Trumah and Kodshim (silent acceptance without speech)
